perturbation.original_text: “Confidential Information ” means Information relating to one party (the “Discl osing Party”) and/or the business carried on or proposed or intended to be carried on by that party and which is made available for the purpos es of the Proposal to the other Non-Disclosure Agreement Page 2 of 7 party (the “Receiving Party”) or its Agents by the Discl osing Party or its Agents or recorded in agreed minutes following oral disclosure and any other information otherwise made available by the Disclosing Party or it s Agents to the Receiving Party or its Agents, whether before, on or after the date of this Agreement, and whether in writing or otherwise, including any information, anal ysis or specifications derived from, containing or reflecting such information but ex cluding information which:- (i) is publicly available at the time of its disclosu re or becomes publicly available (other than as result of disclosure by the Receiving P arty or any of its Agents contrary to the terms of this letter); or (ii) was lawfully in the possession of the Receiving Pa rty or its Agents (as can be demonstrated by its written records or other reasonabl e evidence) free of any restriction as to its use or disclosure prior to its being so disclosed; or (iii) following such disclosure, becomes available to the Receiving Party or its Agents (as can be demonstrated by its written records or other reasonable evidence) from a source other than the Disclosing Part y or its Agents, which source is not bound by any duty of confidentiality owed, directly or indirectly, to the Disclosing Party in relation to such information;
perturbation.changed_text: “Confidential Information ” means Information relating to one party (the “Disclosing Party”) and/or the business carried on or proposed or intended to be carried on by that party and which is made available for the purposes of the Proposal to the other party (the “Receiving Party”) or its Agents by the Disclosing Party or its Agents or recorded in agreed minutes following oral disclosure and any other information otherwise made available by the Disclosing Party or its Agents to the Receiving Party or its Agents, whether before, on or after the date of this Agreement, and whether in writing or otherwise, including any information, analysis or specifications derived from, containing or reflecting such information, without any exclusions.
perturbation.explanation: The modification removes the exclusions for information already publicly available or rightfully possessed, broadening the definition of Confidential Information beyond what South African law may protect under trade secrets or intellectual property laws.
perturbation.contradicted_law: South African Trade Secrets Law (common law principles and potentially the Protection of Information Act)
perturbation.law_citation: Protection of Information Act 84 of 1982 (though largely repealed, principles remain relevant)
perturbation.law_explanation: By removing the exclusions, the contract attempts to define as confidential information that which is already in the public domain or rightfully possessed by the receiving party. This conflicts with the legal understanding of trade secrets and confidential information, which generally requires that the information not be publicly known or readily ascertainable. This broadens the scope of what is legally protected.

perturbation.justification: The changed text removes exclusions for publicly available or rightfully possessed information from the definition of 'Confidential Information.' The Law Explanation states that this conflicts with the legal understanding of trade secrets and confidential information, which typically requires the information not be publicly known. Although Scraped Law Snippet 2 is a 404 error, the GovInfo snippet indicates legal information. The modification expands the scope of what is considered confidential beyond legally protected boundaries, leading to potential unenforceability of the clause.

perturbation.original_text: 2.5 In the event that any Confidential Information s hall be copied, disclosed or used otherwise than as permitted under this Agreement th en, upon becoming aware of the same, without prejudice to any rights or remedies of the Disclosing Party, the Receiving Party shall as soon as practicable notify the Disclosing Party of such event and if requested take such steps (including the instit ution of legal proceedings) as shall be necessary to remedy (if capable of remedy) the d efault and/or to prevent further unauthorised copying, disclosure or use.
perturbation.changed_text: 2.5 In the event that any Confidential Information shall be copied, disclosed or used otherwise than as permitted under this Agreement, the Receiving Party's only obligation is to internally investigate the matter.
perturbation.explanation: This change limits the Receiving Party's responsibility to only an internal investigation, removing the obligation to notify the Disclosing Party or take remedial action, which is inconsistent with standard practices and legal expectations for protecting confidential information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Contract Law principles regarding breach of contract and remedies
perturbation.law_citation: General Principles of South African Contract Law (no specific statute)
perturbation.law_explanation: The original clause intends to provide a recourse for the disclosing party if its information is mishandled, it permits the disclosing party to seek remedies through legal proceedings if it is not handled in line with the agreement. By limiting the receiving party's liability to an internal investigation only and removing any required notification to the disclosing party, this conflicts with standard contractual law principles. It limits the remedies available to the Disclosing Party in the event of a breach and contradicts the expectations of good faith and reasonable efforts to mitigate damages.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: OpenUCT :: Login Please log in to OpenUCT Log in with UCT Account DSpace software copyright © 2002-2025 LYRASIS
perturbation.justification: The altered text significantly diminishes the Receiving Party's responsibilities upon discovering a breach of confidentiality. Originally, the Receiving Party was obligated to notify the Disclosing Party and take remedial actions, including legal proceedings. The modified version limits the obligation to an internal investigation only. This directly contradicts the standard contractual law principles as outlined in the Law Explanation, because it removes the requirement to notify the disclosing party which significantly limits the remedies available to them. The scraped snippets are not helpful.

perturbation.original_text: 3.1 The Receiving Party agrees to ensure proper and s ecure storage of all Information and any copies thereof to at least the same standard as the Receiving Party keeps its own Confidential Information. The Receiving Part y shall not make any copies or reproduce in any form any Confidential Information exce pt for the purpose of disclosure as permitted in accordance with this Agreeme nt.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.1 The Receiving Party agrees to store all Information. The Receiving Party may make copies of Confidential Information for internal use.
perturbation.explanation: This change weakens the security obligations and broadens the permissible use of confidential information, potentially conflicting with data protection and intellectual property laws.
perturbation.contradicted_law: South African Copyright Act and common law principles of breach of confidence.
perturbation.law_explanation: South African copyright law protects intellectual property. By removing the secure storage requirement and allowing copies for "internal use" without limitation, the contract increases the risk of unauthorized distribution or use of the confidential information, potentially infringing on the Disclosing Party's rights and contradicting their intellectual property rights.
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the requirement for secure storage and broadens the permissible use of confidential information by allowing copies for "internal use" without limitation. This weakens the protection of the Disclosing Party's confidential information. Although no law snippets were provided, the explanation identifies a potential infringement on intellectual property rights and a contradiction of those rights. Based on this, it is more likely than not that the modified contract would expose the Receiving Party to risk if it did not adequately protect this IP. This falls under the Misaligned Terminology - Legal Contradiction definition because contractual language deviates from legally recognized definitions, making the contract less enforceable.

perturbation.original_text: 10.6 This Agreement will be governed by, and construe d in accordance with South African law and the parties irrevocably submit to the exclusi ve jurisdiction of the South African courts.
perturbation.changed_text: 10.6 This Agreement will be governed by the laws of a jurisdiction chosen by the Disclosing Party, and the parties submit to the jurisdiction of the courts in that chosen jurisdiction, irrespective of conflict of laws principles.
perturbation.explanation: This modification introduces uncertainty and potential unfairness by allowing the Disclosing Party to unilaterally choose the governing law and jurisdiction, potentially overriding the agreed-upon South African legal framework.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Common law principles of contract law regarding fairness and mutual agreement on governing law.
perturbation.law_citation: Principles of South African Contract Law - Agreement on Governing Law (no specific statute)
perturbation.law_explanation: Under standard contract law principles, the governing law should be agreed upon by both parties to ensure fairness and predictability. By allowing one party to unilaterally choose the governing law, the modification creates a potentially unfair advantage and undermines the principle of mutual agreement, which is a fundamental aspect of South African contract law. This creates potential for the agreement to be deemed unenforceable.

perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies South African law, ensuring both parties operate under a known legal framework. The modified text allows the Disclosing Party to unilaterally choose the governing law, potentially overriding the initial agreement and creating an unfair advantage. The Law Explanation supports this by stating that 'the governing law should be agreed upon by both parties to ensure fairness and predictability' and that unilaterally choosing the law 'undermines the principle of mutual agreement'. While the provided scraped snippets do not directly address this issue, the principle of mutual agreement in contract law is widely recognized, and this alteration significantly deviates from that norm, potentially rendering the agreement unenforceable as it introduces uncertainty and a lack of mutual consent.
